title: SEAI Home Energy Grants 2026: The Complete Irish Homeowner's Guide
author: RetrofitList.ie Editorial Team
summary: Updated for 2026 — every SEAI grant, the exact amounts by house type, who qualifies, and a step-by-step guide to applying. Includes the new heat pump grant of up to €12,500 and recent increases to attic and cavity wall grants.

body.text: SEAI home energy grants are among the most generous residential energy subsidies in Europe — and in 2026, the amounts increased significantly. If you've been putting off a retrofit because of the cost, the numbers have shifted considerably in your favour. This guide covers every available grant, updated to reflect the February and March 2026 changes.

Updated February 2026: Heat pump grants increased to a maximum of €12,500. Attic insulation grants increased to €2,000 for detached homes. Cavity wall grants also increased. From 2 March 2026, homeowners can apply for a grant for two wall measures in the same application.
2026 Grant Amounts: Full Table by Measure and House Type
All grants below are through the Better Energy Homes (BEH) individual grants scheme. Amounts vary by house type — detached homes with more surface area typically receive higher grants.

The heat pump grant breakdown: €6,500 for the heat pump unit, €2,000 for upgrading radiators and pipework, and a new €4,000 renewable heating bonus — total €12,500. This is the single largest individual home energy grant available in Ireland.
Who Qualifies?
To qualify for SEAI Better Energy Homes grants your property must meet all of the following:

items: The home was built and occupied before 2011
You own and live in the property as your principal private residence
The work has not already started (you must apply before work begins)
You use an SEAI-registered contractor for all works
The property is not a new build or a rental property (separate schemes exist for landlords)

body.text: Properties built after 2010 generally already meet modern energy standards and are not eligible. Rental properties may qualify under the Residential Retrofit Scheme — contact SEAI for details.
Enhanced Grants for Lower-Income Households

From March 2026, homeowners on qualifying welfare payments (including Fuel Allowance, Working Family Payment, and Carer's Allowance) qualify for higher grant rates on cavity wall and attic insulation. The fully funded Warmer Homes Scheme provides free energy upgrades to households on certain means-tested payments — no cost at all to the homeowner.
Step-by-Step: How to Apply

items: Check eligibility — confirm your home was built before 2011 and you own and occupy it
Choose your measures — decide which upgrades you want based on your home's needs and BER rating
Find an SEAI registered contractor — use the RetrofitList.ie directory to find contractors in your county
Get at least three quotes — compare on price, timeline, and the contractor's SEAI registration status
Apply for your grant at seai.ie BEFORE work begins — you will receive a grant approval reference number
Have the works completed by your registered contractor
Submit completion documents — your contractor will typically handle this
SEAI pays the grant — directly to your bank account, usually within 4–6 weeks of completion

body.text: Important: You must apply for and receive grant approval BEFORE work begins. Grants cannot be applied for retrospectively. Any work carried out before grant approval is ineligible.
Can You Combine Grants?

Yes — and it's encouraged. You can apply for multiple measures in a single application. From March 2026, you can also apply for two different wall insulation measures (for example, external wall insulation on one elevation and internal on another). There is no overall cap on the total grant amount, though each measure has its own maximum.

If you are planning a whole-home deep retrofit targeting a B2 BER or better, consider using an SEAI-approved One-Stop-Shop provider instead. The One-Stop-Shop route has higher maximum grant levels and the provider manages all paperwork on your behalf.
Find SEAI-registered contractors near you

title: Attic Insulation in Ireland 2026: The Complete Guide
author: Ciarán Murphy, BEng
summary: Everything you need to know about attic insulation in Ireland — what type to use, how thick it should be, what a good installation looks like, how much it costs, and how to claim your SEAI grant of up to €2,000.

body.text: Attic insulation is the single most cost-effective energy upgrade available to Irish homeowners. Up to 25% of heat loss in a typical Irish home escapes through an uninsulated or poorly insulated roof — and fixing it costs less than almost any other measure. With SEAI grants of up to €2,000 and typical installation costs of €800–€2,000, many homeowners break even within 2–3 years.
How Much Heat Are You Losing?

The exact amount depends on your home's age and current insulation levels. Homes built before 1980 often have no attic insulation at all, or thin, degraded material installed in the 1980s that no longer meets current standards. Homes built between 1980 and 2005 typically have 100mm of mineral wool — half the current recommended depth.
What Type of Insulation Is Used?

The vast majority of attic insulation in Irish homes uses mineral wool — either glass wool (softer, yellow) or rock wool (denser, grey-brown). Both perform similarly. They are laid between the joists and then over the top in a second layer, which is the key to achieving full coverage.

Blown-in insulation (loose fill) is used in some cases, particularly where access is difficult or the attic space is complex. Rigid insulation boards are used in flat roofs and certain specialist applications, but are not typical for pitched attic retrofits.
How Thick Does It Need to Be?

Current Irish building regulations (Part L) require a minimum of 300mm total depth for attic insulation retrofits, achieving a U-value of 0.16 W/m²K or better. Most professionals recommend going to 400mm — the marginal cost difference is small and the additional performance is meaningful.
What Should a Good Installation Include?

Before insulation is laid, a competent contractor will carry out a thorough pre-installation inspection. This is non-negotiable — insulating over hidden problems traps them and can cause serious long-term damage.

items: Roof inspection for leaks, damaged timbers, and signs of dampness
Inspection for existing pest infestation (rodents, insects)
Checking that all electrical wiring in the attic is safely routed
Ensuring adequate roof ventilation will be maintained after insulation
Insulating the attic hatch door (this is a significant cold spot if missed)
Insulating around but not under the cold water tank (it needs warmth to prevent freezing)
Lagging all exposed water pipes
Installing a vapour control layer where required

body.text: Red flag: if a contractor does not inspect the attic before quoting, or does not mention the attic hatch and water tank in their specification, look elsewhere. A thorough pre-installation check is the mark of a professional installation.
What About Attic Storage?

Many homeowners use their attic for storage, which creates a challenge — you need 300–400mm of insulation depth, but you also need to walk on the floor. The solution is a raised boarding system: a specialist framework that sits on top of the joists and insulation, allowing full insulation depth while preserving usable storage space. Expect to pay €300–€600 extra for this, depending on the area being boarded.
How Much Does It Cost?

For many house types, the SEAI grant now covers the full cost of a standard installation — meaning attic insulation can be essentially free for qualifying homeowners. Always get a minimum of three quotes before committing.
How to Claim the SEAI Grant

items: Apply online at seai.ie before any work begins — you must have grant approval first
Receive your grant approval reference number (usually within a few days)
Engage your SEAI-registered contractor and have the work carried out
Your contractor submits the completion certificate and supporting documentation to SEAI
SEAI pays the grant directly to your bank account — typically within 4–6 weeks

body.text: Only SEAI-registered contractors are eligible for grant-funded work. Always verify your contractor's current registration at seai.ie before signing any agreement or making any payment.
Find attic insulation contractors near you

title: Irish Home Improvement Grants 2026: Local Authority Schemes Explained
author: Aoife Brennan
summary: Beyond SEAI, Ireland's local authorities offer a range of home improvement grants that can significantly reduce your retrofit costs. This guide covers every major scheme — who qualifies, how much is available, and how to apply in your county.

body.text: Most Irish homeowners know about SEAI grants — but far fewer are aware of the substantial local authority schemes that can sit alongside them. In some cases, stacking national and local grants can reduce the cost of a retrofit by 80–90%, or make certain upgrades entirely free. This guide covers every major scheme currently available.
The Four National Local Authority Schemes

These four schemes are available from all 31 local authorities across Ireland. While the maximum amounts are set nationally, individual local authorities may set lower income thresholds or additional criteria.
1. Housing Aid for Older Persons (HAOP)

Available to homeowners aged 66 or over with household income below the threshold (typically €30,000–€40,000 depending on the local authority). Maximum grant: €8,000. Covers general repairs and improvements including roofing, windows, heating systems, and insulation. This is the most widely used local authority home improvement grant in Ireland.
2. Housing Adaptation Grant for People with a Disability

Maximum grant: €30,000. Available to homeowners who need to adapt their home due to disability or long-term illness. While not exclusively for energy upgrades, heating system improvements are commonly funded where there is a medical need. Income-tested, with the grant amount calculated as a percentage based on household income.
3. Mobility Aids Grant

Maximum grant: €6,000. Targeted at people with mobility difficulties, primarily for grab rails, stair lifts, accessible bathrooms, and level access. Less relevant for energy upgrades but can be combined with other schemes.
4. Warmer Homes Scheme (SEAI-funded, locally delivered)

Not strictly a local authority scheme, but delivered in partnership with local authorities. Provides fully funded energy upgrades — free of charge — to homeowners on qualifying means-tested welfare payments (including Fuel Allowance, Jobseeker's Allowance, One-Parent Family Payment, and others). A typical Warmer Homes upgrade includes attic insulation, wall insulation, draught proofing, and heating controls. Waiting lists currently stand at 12–18 months in most areas.
Can You Stack Local Authority and SEAI Grants?

Yes — in many cases you can combine local authority grants with SEAI Better Energy Homes grants, provided the combined amount does not exceed 100% of the total project cost. This is the most effective way to minimise out-of-pocket expenditure. For example, an older homeowner combining the Housing Aid for Older Persons grant with SEAI attic and wall insulation grants could fund almost the entire cost of a meaningful energy upgrade.

Stacking tip: Apply for your SEAI grant first (it pays a fixed amount), then apply to your local authority for any remaining balance. The local authority calculation usually accounts for other grants received.
Local Authority Pilot and Community Schemes
Several local authorities run their own energy efficiency pilot programmes beyond the national schemes. These change regularly and vary significantly by county. Recent examples include:

items: Dublin City Council: cavity wall insulation programmes in certain estates
Cork City and County Council: energy upgrade schemes for low-income homeowners
Limerick City and County Council: community retrofit pilots in designated areas
Various county councils: deep retrofit support through the Sustainable Energy Communities (SEC) programme

body.text: The Sustainable Energy Communities programme is worth particular attention. SEAI provides funding and a dedicated project manager to communities — estates, streets, or rural areas — that form a group and pursue whole-area upgrades collectively. Group purchasing typically reduces contractor costs by 10–20%, and the community structure can access additional grant supports.
How to Find Out What's Available in Your County

Local authority schemes are managed by housing departments and change more frequently than national programmes. The most reliable approach is to contact your county or city council's housing department directly — by phone or via their website's online enquiry form.

items: Search '[your county] council housing grants' for current information
Call the local authority housing grants line — staff can confirm current eligibility and amounts
Ask your SEAI-registered contractor — experienced contractors will be familiar with local schemes in their area
Check gov.ie/en/service/housing-grants for the national scheme overview

body.text: Find registered retrofit contractors in your county

title: Heat Pumps in Ireland 2026: Costs, Grants, Suitability and What to Expect
author: RetrofitList.ie Editorial Team
summary: The heat pump grant jumped to €12,500 in February 2026, making this the largest single home energy grant in Ireland. This guide covers how heat pumps work in Irish homes, whether yours is suitable, realistic costs, and what to ask an installer.

body.text: Air-to-water heat pumps have become the dominant replacement heating system for Irish homes moving away from oil and gas. Installations grew 38% in 2024, and the February 2026 increase of the heat pump grant to €12,500 — the largest home energy grant now available in Ireland — is expected to accelerate that further. But heat pumps are not suitable for every home, and the technology is frequently misunderstood. This guide sets out what you actually need to know.
How Does a Heat Pump Work in an Irish Home?

An air-to-water heat pump extracts heat energy from outdoor air — even at temperatures as low as -15°C — and uses it to heat water, which then circulates through radiators or underfloor heating. It works on the same thermodynamic principle as a refrigerator, but in reverse.

The key metric is the Coefficient of Performance (COP): for every 1 unit of electricity consumed, a modern heat pump delivers 3–4 units of heat. Running costs therefore depend heavily on your electricity tariff. With a heat pump-specific electricity tariff (now offered by most Irish suppliers), running costs can be competitive with gas and significantly lower than oil.
Is My Home Suitable?

This is the most important question to answer before considering a heat pump. The technology works best in well-insulated homes with adequate heat distribution. A poorly insulated home with undersized radiators will result in a heat pump that runs continuously and is expensive to operate.

SEAI recommendation: your home should achieve at least a C1 BER rating before installing a heat pump for best results. This typically means having adequate attic and wall insulation in place first. Your installer should carry out a full heat loss assessment (required for SEAI grant approval) before specifying the system.
Types of Heat Pump Available in Ireland

items: Air-to-Water (most common): outdoor unit extracts heat from air, heats water for radiators and hot water cylinder. Suitable for most Irish homes with wet heating systems.
Ground Source (horizontal): pipes buried in garden extract heat from the ground. More efficient but requires significant outdoor space (typically 3× the house footprint).
Ground Source (vertical): borehole drilled to ~100m depth. High efficiency, minimal land requirement, but high installation cost.
Exhaust Air to Water: extracts heat from ventilated air inside the building. Suitable for very airtight, well-ventilated homes only.
Air-to-Air: heats air directly (like a reverse air conditioner). Does not heat hot water. Rarely used in Ireland as a primary heating system.

body.text: What Does It Cost in 2026?

The €12,500 maximum grant applies when all three components are included: the heat pump itself (€6,500), radiator and pipework upgrades (€2,000), and the new renewable heating bonus (€4,000). Not all installations will attract all three components — your installer will specify which elements qualify.
What to Ask Your Heat Pump Installer

items: Are you currently on the SEAI heat pump installer register? (Ask to see their SEAI registration number)
Will you carry out a full heat loss assessment (MCS or CIBSE) before sizing the system?
What COP do you expect this system to achieve in my home, and what assumptions does that use?
What radiator upgrades do you recommend, and why?
What electricity tariff do you recommend for running this system?
What is the warranty on the unit, and who provides after-sales support?
Can you provide references from similar installations in my area?

body.text: Running Costs: What to Realistically Expect

Annual running costs for a heat pump in a well-insulated Irish home of around 120m² typically fall between €900 and €1,600 depending on the electricity tariff, the home's insulation level, and usage patterns. This compares favourably with oil (typically €1,400–€2,200/year) and gas (€1,000–€1,600/year at current rates).

The key variable is the electricity tariff. Several Irish suppliers now offer heat pump-specific tariffs with lower off-peak rates. Setting up your heat pump to run primarily on lower-rate electricity (typically overnight) through a time-of-use tariff can reduce annual running costs by 20–30%.
Find SEAI-registered heat pump installers near you

title: External Wall Insulation in Ireland: The Complete Guide
author: Siobhán O'Neill
summary: External Wall Insulation (EWI) is the most impactful single upgrade for solid-walled Irish homes. This guide covers the planning rules, the different EWI systems available, realistic costs, SEAI grants of up to €8,000, and what to expect during installation.

body.text: External Wall Insulation (EWI) transforms the thermal performance of an Irish home by wrapping the outside walls in an insulating layer — typically 100–150mm thick — covered in a new render finish. It is the most impactful upgrade available for older solid-walled homes, which make up the majority of Ireland's pre-1980 housing stock. For these homes, EWI can cut heat loss through walls by up to 80%.
Do You Need Planning Permission?

For the vast majority of Irish homes, external wall insulation is classified as exempted development — no planning application is required. Under the Planning and Development Regulations 2022, EWI is exempt provided:

items: The additional thickness does not exceed 250mm on any elevation
The work does not involve replacing windows or doors (replacing windows alongside EWI is a separate, common-sense decision but is not part of the exempt works)
The property is not a protected structure or in an Architectural Conservation Area (ACA)

body.text: Check your property status before starting: if your home is a protected structure or within an ACA (common in historic town centres, Georgian areas, and many rural estates), you will need planning permission. Check your local authority's planning map or contact their planning department for a pre-application consultation — it's free and avoids costly surprises.

Even where planning permission is not required, all EWI work must comply with Part L (energy) and Part B (fire safety) of the Building Regulations. Your contractor is responsible for compliance, and SEAI-funded work is subject to inspection.
Types of EWI Systems
There are several EWI systems in use across Ireland. Your contractor will specify the most appropriate based on your wall construction, exposure to wind-driven rain, and aesthetic preferences.

rows: Most standard brick and block homes
High fire resistance, solid stone walls
Where maximum performance in minimal thickness is needed

body.text: Bead injection is technically a cavity wall measure (not EWI), but is included here because it is the most cost-effective option for homes with existing unfilled cavities. It involves drilling small holes in the outer leaf of the wall and injecting polystyrene beads under pressure — walls are left unchanged externally.
What Does EWI Cost?

EWI costs vary considerably depending on wall area, the complexity of the building (bay windows, reveals, decorative features all add labour), the system chosen, and access requirements. Always get a minimum of three quotes from SEAI-registered contractors.
What Happens During Installation?

items: Site preparation — scaffolding erected, window sills and external fittings removed
Wall preparation — any defects in the existing wall repaired; surface cleaned
Insulation boards fixed — mechanically fixed and/or adhesive-bonded to the wall
Reinforcing mesh — embedded in base coat around windows, corners, and over boards
Base coat — applied over the full surface and mesh
Final render or finish — applied in one or two coats depending on system
Window sills replaced (extended to account for the new wall thickness)
Scaffolding removed and site cleaned

body.text: A typical semi-detached house takes 5–10 working days depending on weather. The internal dimensions of the house are unaffected — insulation is entirely external.
Common Mistakes to Watch For

items: Insufficient insulation depth — anything below 100mm will underperform; 120–150mm is standard for Irish conditions
Poor detailing around windows and doors — cold bridges here negate much of the benefit
Not treating the roof overhangs — the junction between the EWI and the soffit needs careful detailing
Using inappropriate systems in high-exposure coastal locations — not all renders are suitable for exposed sites
Skipping ventilation consideration — in very airtight homes, EWI can worsen condensation if ventilation is not addressed

body.text: Always ask your contractor for a full specification document before work begins — including the system manufacturer, insulation type and thickness, render type, and U-value achieved. A reputable contractor will provide this without being asked.
Find SEAI-registered EWI contractors near you

title: SEAI One-Stop-Shop Retrofit: The Complete Guide for Irish Homeowners
author: Pádraig Walsh
summary: The One-Stop-Shop model handles your entire retrofit from first call to final BER certificate — grant paperwork included. This guide explains how it works, how the grants compare, what it really costs, and how to choose the right provider from Ireland's 32 approved OSS companies.

body.text: The SEAI One-Stop-Shop (OSS) model was introduced in 2022 alongside the National Home Energy Upgrade Scheme. The premise is simple: instead of managing multiple contractors, multiple grant applications, and a complex project yourself, you engage a single SEAI-approved provider who takes responsibility for the entire process — from the initial home energy assessment to the final BER certificate.
There are currently 32 SEAI-approved One-Stop-Shop providers in Ireland. This guide explains how the model works, whether it suits your situation, and how to choose between the providers available.
What Does a One-Stop-Shop Actually Do?

items: Initial consultation — you contact the OSS provider and discuss your home and goals
Technical assessment — a BER assessor (usually employed or contracted by the OSS) visits your home and carries out a full energy assessment
Retrofit plan — the OSS produces a tailored upgrade plan showing which measures will achieve your target BER rating, with a full cost and grant breakdown
Grant application — the OSS applies for all SEAI grants on your behalf
Project management — the OSS coordinates all tradespeople and manages the works programme
Works completed — by SEAI-registered sub-contractors managed by the OSS
Quality assurance — the OSS arranges all SEAI inspections and sign-offs
Final BER certificate — issued on completion

body.text: Throughout this process, you deal with one company and one point of contact. The SEAI pays all grants directly to the OSS provider, and you pay only the net balance after grants.
How Do the Grants Compare to the DIY Route?

The One-Stop-Shop route provides access to higher grant levels under the National Home Energy Upgrade Scheme (NHEUS) compared to the standard Better Energy Homes individual grants. The key difference is the Deep Retrofit Grant, available exclusively through the OSS route:

rows: Same individual grants plus Deep Retrofit Grant

body.text: The Deep Retrofit Grant provides an additional €8,000 to €20,000 for homes achieving a significant improvement in BER rating (typically from D or below to B2 or better). This makes the OSS route substantially more generous for homeowners planning a comprehensive upgrade.
What Does It Actually Cost?

A typical whole-home deep retrofit through an OSS provider — covering external wall insulation, attic insulation, heat pump, and mechanical ventilation — costs in the region of €45,000–€70,000 before grants. With SEAI grants (including the deep retrofit top-up), the net cost to the homeowner is typically €15,000–€35,000 depending on the measures, house type, and grant eligibility.

OSS providers charge a project management fee, typically 10–15% of total project cost. This is legitimate and represents the coordination, grant administration, and quality assurance services they provide. It should be clearly itemised in your quote.
OSS vs. DIY: Which Is Right for You?

rows: Higher — you manage contractors and grants

body.text: How to Choose an OSS Provider

With 32 approved providers in Ireland, ranging from large national utilities (Electric Ireland Superhomes, Bord Gáis Energy, SSE Airtricity) to specialist regional operators, there is genuine choice. Here is what to look for:

items: SEAI approval — confirm the provider is on the current SEAI OSS register before engaging
Geographic coverage — not all providers operate nationwide; check they work in your county
References — ask for two or three references from completed projects similar in scale to yours
Sub-contractor transparency — ask who they use for insulation, heat pumps, and ventilation, and whether those contractors are SEAI registered
Project management fee — ask for this to be itemised clearly in the quote
Timeline — ask for a realistic timeline from assessment to completion
Aftercare — what support is provided if issues arise after the works are complete?

body.text: Key Questions to Ask at Your First Meeting

items: What BER rating do you expect to achieve, and what measures are needed to get there?
What is the total cost, and what grants are you applying for on my behalf?
What is your project management fee, and what does it include?
Who are your sub-contractors for each measure, and are they SEAI registered?
What is the expected timeline from assessment to completion certificate?
What happens if costs increase during the project?
Can I see a completed example of your work?

body.text: Find SEAI-approved One-Stop-Shop providers near you
